[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will not be put through.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

(The image? I've finished the sequence of my published books; while I decide what I want to do next, I have some memes to share.)

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com. 

With that said, have at it!

comments.content:
So, I had a dream last night and I think it is related to the very recent experience I mentioned elsewhere. In the dream, I was in some kind of wooded area with some friends. Not friends in real life, just a sense in my dream that the men with me were friends. Anyway, a monster comes out of nowhere and starts killing us. We were all armed with conventional weapons: guns and knives. The entity made short order of us, and I began running like heck, finally getting into a clearing. The entity was right behind me, and I turned around to face it. I decided fighting was pointless, and I dropped my weapons at its feet, then knelt down for the killing blow. The entity walked away, sparing me. I then woke up, still feeling a palpable sense of terror.

I got the powerful impression from this dream that the work I am called to requires me to surrender my real world weapons collection, save for a small obsidian blade knife that I bought on a whim last Fall but now I’m thinking it is meant to be a primary ceremonial tool. I suspect it relates also to my spirit guide, who belonged to a pacifistic religion during life. I know that dream interpretation is not something you really do, but if the commentariat has any thoughts, it would be most welcome.

Are there any notable occult works on non-violence? I’d be interested in diving into what the Western occult traditions have to say, though my metaphysics and ethics is increasingly Eastern (for whatever reason, Jain Ahimsa teachings seem to be part of some personal synchronicities.).

Such strange times.

Second, I don't happen to know of anything that discusses nonviolence in the Western occult traditions, but others may be able to help you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Well, that does sound like a truly wonderful dream to have experienced! I find the most insightful dream readings tend to come from interpreting every aspect of one's dream to be a metaphor for some aspect of one's self. That wilderness (similar to an ocean or a cavern) could be some wild, unexplored part of your subconscious/unconscious.

How many friends were you with there? If three, I would lean towards considering them to be those aspects of your soul that dwell on each of the planes we inhabit. If four, then one of the five of you would likely be your higher self, to round out the soul’s mix. If your posse does map onto various constituent parts of your soul, then that was quite a potent dream, since your nemesis was fully able to weaken and isolate you from your greater soul until you chose to turn, face, and honor the monster that had welled up within you.

Generically, that monster is a dead ringer for your Shadow; however, the transformative power of any dream lies in unpacking its specificity. What have you been working through recently? What have you been fighting? What has seemed too wild and threatening to face or acknowledge? What have you resisted exploring within? Alternatively, what did the monster look like, act like, who or what did it remind you of? Likewise, how did you feel when you knelt down to it, when it walked away, when you woke up, and when you now reflect over the dream?

Something magical shifted within you last night. Can you sense what is beginning to move into new alignments? Which plates are drifting; which glaciers calving; which flows might be changing their course? Kneeling down before your dreaded enemy and offering up your weapons of resistance naturally transforms that feared monster into a familiar mentor. You've been traveling together for such a long time, you might as well begin availing yourself of its hard-earned wisdom!

— Christophe

replies.content:
Including myself, there were seven people in the group of friends attacked by the monster. My normal go to for interpreting something like this dream would be Jungian concepts. That said, I've done considerable Shadow, and at this point Anima, work. I've gotten to know my Shadow very well.

The monster itself was serpentine in appearance. Humanoid serpentine, if that makes sense. It was very fast, very brutal, and quite merciless. The other six men in my group were quickly and brutally shredded by the monster. It pursued me almost without any noise. When I reached the open clearing in the dream, I thought at first I had outrun it. I had not, and it was there behind me.

I'm not sure how relevant this will be but ever since I was a little boy, I've had dreams where someone or something was attacking me, and I'd have a revolver on me. But whenever I tried to fire the revolver at the threat, each and every bullet was a dud. A few rare dreams have occurred where the weapon did fire, with minimal effect on the attacker. In this my latest dream, I never actually fired at the creature as I couldn't get a clear shot. It killed my friends very, very quickly (and was impervious to their weapons, anyway). After the short but brutal slaughter, I ran off and it caught up to me. In the dream, the dropping of my weapons at its feet felt like an offering to it, and it felt like it placated it (so long as I didn't pick up the weapons again).

As for what I've been working through as of late, well, that's quite the story. Not easy to fit in a coherent post. I've been experiencing increasing communications from spirits, and then the same day this dream happened (or, well, happened a few hours before I fell asleep and then had this dream) I had the most bizarre experience of my life. I had a visionary experience where I was dropped into the underworld, had some kind of painful ritual performed with a knife, had a large snake crawl inside me, and then I dissolved into the serpent, then transformed back into a man, and then I was returned to the surface world to encounter a large eagle.

Like I said, very bizarre, and it can't be a coincidence that the dream followed this vision.

Spitballing, but:

> the dropping of my weapons at its feet felt like an offering to it, and it felt like it placated it (so long as I didn't pick up the weapons again).

1. In international relations theory, there is a concept called the "security dilemma" or "Thucydides trap", where acquiring destructive power to prevent future attacks from feared others who have power provokes others to fearfully either attack, if they have power, or acquire power if they don't. In this interpretation, the monster wouldn't represent a feared other so much as the entire dynamic.

2. As 1., but for a vaguer concept of operating on a metaphorical plane on which that kind of violence, however imagined as self-defensive it may be at the time, exists.

3. Something about the way you phrased that puts me in mind of the monster acting that way because if considered that particular kind of armed violence or prospect of armed violence to be a pollution.

replies.content:
This is embarrassing, but it would be remiss of me to not note:

> The monster itself was serpentine in appearance. Humanoid serpentine, if that makes sense.

> It was very fast

> very brutal, and quite merciless.

> I'm certain it is Mesoamerican. That much does seem clear to me.

This lines up strikingly with a splice of the descriptions of Koraidon and Miraidon (from korai "ancient" / mirai "future", raido loanword for "ride", and don Gr. "tooth" as metonymy for a dinosaur species), the two mascot monster species of the games Pokémon Scarlet / Violet respectively. "Very fast" lines up with the monsters' mobility and speed; they have two signature attack techniques based on colliding with an opponent at extreme speed. "Brutal" lines up with the primal Koraidon being described in its opposite game's monster index as "far burlier and more ferocious" than their common base species, and "merciless" lines up with the robotic Miraidon being correspondingly described as "far more ruthless and powerful". Both monsters have Mesoamerican-serpent-style large eyes attached to the sides of blunt heads and dramatic scute-like marks, and Koraidon also has feathers (Miraidon instead has plasma forming plumage-like shapes). The two games are set in a region mostly resembling and referencing the Iberian peninsula, but with some Mesoamerican influences and references, notably the Galápagos islands and the Chicxulub impact site (due to the games' background themes of evolutionary parallels, alternate evolutionary histories, and extinction events). The most obvious discrepant point is that both monsters strongly resemble two-wheeled vehicles (which is partly a reference to Spain's biking culture and partly a reimagining of the series' traditional bicycle).

While the most visible Koraidon / Miraidon character in their respective game is protective toward the player character trainer and other humans (due to complicated circumstances involving a trained monster of the base bicycle-like dragon species and
(click to drop down ending spoilers)an evolution-harnessing device that's ambiguously either a time machine, a parallel-universe exchange machine, or a hyperstition machine
), the endgame antagonist also commands a monster of the same species, who is directly specified as having slain a human — one of only perhaps a dozen identified such monster characters across all 30 years of the Pokémon franchise (though in this case more in an equivalent of manslaughter than murder).

While I'm on synchronicites, in the other threads:

> as though you wanted to know if owning a bicycle was incompatible with listening to old Jethro Tull albums

> the last stone of the central tower of Gaudi's Sagrada Familia was laid on the exact day of the conjunction

Besides a bicycle time-paradox serpent monster, one of the other prominent monsters that the player can receive at the beginning of the game is a fire-touched crocodile whose evolutionary sequence develops mariachi-like and alebrije-like or trencadís-like design elements—trencadís is an artistic medium and style most prominently associated with Antoni Gaudí, especially his dragon sculpture in Parc Güell in Barcelona but also visible on some of the spires of Sagrada Familia. The last evolution stage of this monster's line is called Skeledirge, which adds Día de Muertos-related elements like a calavera pattern down its face and snout. The crocodile's mariachi theme develops into coloration patterns sort of resembling the outfit of a Mexican vocalist, and it acquires a symbiotic plover-like bird made of ghostly fire. As part of its signature attack technique, it gets up on two legs to sing and the bird turns into a microphone to pick up its crooning. I don't know, maybe that isn't quite enough elements to make a synchronicity, since crooners are hardly at all the same thing as prog rockers.

replies.content:
I've had multiple dreams over the last few days that I woke from feeling that it was significant, and having a message in my head that I felt needed to be written down.
For me, when that happens, I ask myself if the message was with me when I woke or if it came after reflection on the dream. If I truly woke up with it (sometimes not even in my voice) then I give it more credence, and entertain acting upon it. I also am cautious about acting quickly and generally ask for signs of confirmation, and then follow up with divination.

Not directly western occultism sources...
Quaker tradition is explicitly non-violent and has produced quite a bit of writing on the topic. As a religion, it also has interesting structures and practices that are less opposed to outside practices like occultism than many other christian religions.

Aikido was founded as peaceful martial art in which the primary goal is protection of one's own self through maintaining balance and structure, with a close secondary goal of protecting one's opponent, using techniques that are intended to minimize violence from both ends. There are multiple philosophical books written by the founder and his students that expound upon that. The founder was a military leader and led a violent life before moving onto this, but many of the ideals and ideas have been helpful to me. I've also incorporated multiple of aikido's body mechanics, energetic principles, and techniques into my occult practices and they've influenced my understanding. Many modern dojos aren't great at functional self protection, but there are a few out there that succeed there. The philosophy is accessible to anyone though.

Judo comes from the same foundation and is called the gentle art, but modern judo is very much a sport and more violent for it. The original writings are interesting though. Jujitsu is similar, with foundational principles that intended to be able to incapacitate an attacker with as little harm as possible, but it's popularity in MMA has shifted it quite a bit.

Wishing you well!

comments.title: Is bridal mysticism compatible with ceremonial magic?
comments.content:
Greetings from Seattle!
Something I’ve been trying to figure out for a while now is how, if at all, does bridal mysticism (by the likes of Hadewijch, Porete, Mechthild, even Rumi from Sufism can work here) fit in to a Golden Dawn framework.

My question is twofold:
1. Are the two at odds with one another? Could they be complementary? Can a bridal mystic, one who has encountered the Beloved, also practice ceremonial magic? Are there predecessors to the GD with which this mysticism is derived from? Is there any obvious relationship with one another? Am I comparing apples to oranges here?
2. How would the “contact” made between the lover and Beloved/devotee and Divine, be described by the Golden Dawn tradition, and why such contact occurs?

P.S. Just listened to your latest appearance on Plant Cunning podcast - love the idea of “tactical” astrology for daily use! I do hope that publisher ends up snatching up your book!

replies.title: Re: Is bridal mysticism compatible with ceremonial magic?
replies.content:
1) Magic and mysticism are radically different -- it's not apples and oranges, it's apples and orangutans! They aren't incompatible, they simply operate on entirely different planes of existence. It's as though you wanted to know if owning a bicycle was incompatible with listening to old Jethro Tull albums.

2) It wouldn't be, because the Golden Dawn system teaches magic, not mysticism. Your relationship to the Divine is not a subject about which magic has anything to say. Your relationship to your own higher potentials as a human being? That's quite another matter, but that's not really something that mysticism deals with in any direct fashion.

That being the case, I'd encourage you to pursue both, each according to its own rules and principles. You clearly have a good knowledge of the mystical literature, very likely better than mine, so I won't presume to offer advice.

replies.title: Re: Is bridal mysticism compatible with ceremonial magic?
replies.content:
There is an interesting instance of symbolism I want to raise here as part of this thread.

Eliphaz Levi's drawing of Baphomet is the model for the Devil in many modern tarots. The upward pointing arm says Solve, the downward arm says Coagula.

If you look at the Oswald Wirth drawing of the Devil, the arms are reversed. The downward arm is Solve, the upward is Coagula.

I would suggest that Levi's Baphomet is Magic, and Wirth's Devil is Mysticism.

I could be wrong, but at the very least the difference in symbolism is worth quite a bit of meditation.

replies.title: Re: Is bridal mysticism compatible with ceremonial magic?
replies.content: I've seen the terms magic and mysticism used almost synonymously quite a bit. Is this simply a contemporary Western hangup, perhaps stemming from James Frazier's insistence that magic is simply a primitive form of religion, or is there something deeper to it?

replies.title: Re: Is bridal mysticism compatible with ceremonial magic?
replies.content: I see it as one offshoot of the utter ignorance and indifference with which most modern intellectuals treat every kind of spirituality other than their own, if they have one.

replies.title: Re: Is bridal mysticism compatible with ceremonial magic?
replies.content:
In the case of bridal mysticism, specifically, I can see our host's point about it being a very different thing from magic. But mysticism as a whole encompasses many different approaches, some of which, it seems to me, have significant points of contact with some approaches to magic.

A book that has influenced my understanding and practice of mysticism is Jess Byron Hollenback's "Mysticism: Experience, Response, and Empowerment" (U. Penn. Press). He studies the "...cross-cultural significance of recollective practices (methods of one-pointedly focusing attention) within the totality of the lives of mystics. ...not only do recollective practices generate mystical states of awareness, but they also play a critical role in generating the extraordinary phenomena that often accompany those states of consciousness. ...this book contains a lengthy analysis of a common...accompaniment of recollective practice and mystical experience, a phenomenon that I have called the "empowerment" of a mystic's imagination, thoughts, emotions, and volitions. ...Under conditions of recollective empowerment, these faculties not only objectify themselves so that they form the visionary landscape that the mystic perceives, but they also exhibit noetic possibilities, that is, they can sometimes function as supernormal channels of perception, locomotion, and communication."

The author, unlike many academic scholars of mysticism, pays careful attention to the practices of shamanism and similar tribal religious / occult practices from around the world as part of what he considers mysticism, along with the study of more 'classical' forms of mysticism.

To me, the passage I quoted above exhibits some significant similarities to Eliphas Levi's descriptions of magical practice, as well as Dion Fortune's definition of magic. I wonder if perhaps the difference between mysticism and magic is less about methods and abilities, and more about how the results are interpreted and used.

Winifred

replies.title: Re: Is bridal mysticism compatible with ceremonial magic?
replies.content: OP here - I could see how mystical practice could rhyme with magical practice, and thank you for that book rec I will definitely be checking it out. IIRC Dion Fortune (of whom I am a fan!) also distinguishes the mystic apart from magician since their end goals were different. Still, magicians can have mystic experiences, I’ve just not heard of (bridal) mystics becoming ceremonial magicians or getting into the occult as a natural consequence of Divine encounter but surely it’s happened before? I don’t know.

replies.title: Re: Is bridal mysticism compatible with ceremonial magic?
replies.content: Thank so much John. So sorry this is very late. I had a suspicion that the two were not necessarily related, not even in a great-grand-uncle-thrice-removed sort of way. Even if I broadened the question to whether or not Christian mysticism and Christian occultism overlap they’re just two different subjects altogether. But I am relieved that I don’t have to set either one at odds with another.

replies.title: Re: Is bridal mysticism compatible with ceremonial magic?
replies.content: There's no opposition between them, nor any necessary connection; down through the centuries a great many people have practiced one, or the other, or both. Go ye therefore and do whichever of these works for you.

St. Joseph (that Joseph, Jesus' stepdad) is very well know as the saint patron of both families and blue collar workers. But something I just found out is that he's also the saint patron of realtors! Seeking his intercession would be a traditional way Catholics would approach this matter.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) No, because it uses a very different set of symbolism for the Tree. Just as it's not a good idea to try to learn two different foreign languages at the same time, because you'll get them mixed up, it's not a good idea to study two different versions of the Cabala at the same time.

2) They use different energies in different ways. Once you've been doing ceremonial magic for a while and your subtle body has built up some resilience, you can do workings of both kinds at different times, but in the early stages of training I really do recommend one or the other.

3) Here again, you gain nothing by trying to do two things at once -- it's as though you tried to drive north and south at the same time. Do one, finish it, and then do the other.

comments.title: The musical mystery of Rosslyn Chapel
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

comments.content:
Is the mystery and mystique surrounding magic on the whole a good thing or bad thing?

On the one hand, it adds a bit of spice and vibrancy to the practice, but on the other it's part of what makes non-practitioners fear and persecute it. As for attracting people to the path, it seems like a wash: for every person who should be on the path who was attracted by the mystique, how many people who shouldn't be on the path does it draw?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As I see it, it's not the mystery and the the mystique that makes people fear and persecute magicians. It's the hard fact that some people use magic to hurt, dominate, and exploit other people. The more of this takes place, the more likely persecution becomes -- which is one of the reasons why the recent fad for evil magic is so stupid and so counterproductive.

I see the mystique as highly counterproductive, for what it's worth. It draws a lot of people who have no business practicing magic, and chases off a lot of intelligent people who roll their eyes at the woo-woo.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Is it possible to have magic without the mystery? The practice fundamentally works with forces which we have limited ability to perceive, and many of the more advanced practices actively work with beings above the human plane of existence. Granting our society tends towards blindness to spiritual phenomena, you still can't really expect Joe Bob on the street to interact with magic as anything but a mystery until he does the work to pull back the curtains. Even then, human nature can only grasp so much of the picture.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's quite fair, of course. Though to use an already overused cliche, I barely know how electricity works but that doesn't stop me from turning on the lights. Similarly my understanding of how some of my medications works is no more concrete than my understanding of how a geomantic gamahe works.

Part of my thinking behind my comment was this: there's no hard dividing line between what is and isn't magic. Is it magic when a blacksmith produces a blade that doesn't rust or break nearly as easily as most and nobody but him and a few other grandmaster smiths knows the secret? Is it divination when meteorologists run models to predict the weather over the coming week?

(Strange as it may seem, there isn't much daylight between measuring a chaotic system like the atmosphere to feed into a model and the various techniques used to generate pseudorandom numbers on a computer to turn into Tarot cards.)

So while I doubt the average person would be doing ritual magic (nor should they), I could easily see a future where various natural magic techniques were widely known and employed.

comments.title: Pet Euthenasia
comments.content:
Hello and thank you for this forum.

I have an aging cat who's an indoor/outdoor rescue, and the end of his life is now a subject I'm pondering. My last cat had age related health problems, wound up in the vet's office with failing kidneys, and they called me while I was at work to tell me they needed to euthanize him. I agreed but felt tremendous guilt afterwards for allowing him to die in some strange room surrounded by strangers.

I'm attempting to avoid a repeat for my beloved cat this time by planning ahead. My question for you is if the euthanasia of pets/animals is ever addressed in occult teachings? My impulse here is to comfort a dying pet, and if/when it's clear the pet is just going to suffer to talk to the pet, explain things, say a prayer, and shoot the pet to end it quickly and painlessly. I do think that my cat would understand. I've never personally done this, but I have hunted, fished, and worked on farms where animals are slaughtered so the general concept is not foreign to me.

Pets fall into a middle ground between hunting/slaughter for food, where a bullet is obviously the thing to do, vs human life, where bullets are obviously out but palliative care is the minimum accepted treatment. I just can't afford a $1000+ vet bill to prolong the life of an ailing cat by two weeks, as happened last time. It seems cruel and bad for everyone involved to put an ailing pet in some strange vet's office until it's far enough along that the stranger kills your pet for you. What is your perspective on these matters?

replies.title: Re: Pet Euthenasia
replies.content:
I'm horrified to hear that that's how your vet handled it. When my last pet got too sick to make it -- she was an 18-year-old Siamese with kidney failure -- they waited until we could get to the clinic, and then I held the cat while the vet used the needle. I wonder if you could arrange something similar.

The occult literature known to me doesn't specifically discuss this, but it does talk about the importance of compassion toward all beings, very much including animals.

replies.title: Re: Pet Euthenasia
replies.content: I second this. I've had to put down two cats, and in both cases a vet made a housecall while we (and the kids) held them and petted them. It wasn't cheap, but it wasn't $1000, so I'm sure they could do the same in-office for less.

replies.title: Re: Pet Euthenasia
replies.content:
You don't say whether your last cat was an indoor/outdoor cat. In my experience, such animals know when their time is near, and then find a favorite spot outdoors to pass on. Dreams/or astral cat type noises/hints then let you know where to find their body.

-Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: Pet Euthenasia
replies.content:
I have put an end to a lot of living things between hunting and raising livestock and, in an odd turn of my life, I have actually become "the guy" that people get to come put down their animals when can't handle doing it themselves and can't afford the vet. Where we are at, if you can even get to one at all, the vets are all preposterously expensive.

The odd turn is due to the fact that I really don't enjoy killing things. I think that all life has value. I don't like cutting down trees. Heck, I don't like smashing spiders. I'm the weird guy who will stop in the middle of my walk and fish the worm out of the road and toss him in the grass. (You never know, the great hand intervening inexplicably from above! Maybe that'll come back around to me at an auspicious moment!).

I have killed a lot of stuff though and I have put down people's pets and a few years ago I put down one of my beloved cats. In the case of the cat and other pets I always just shoot them. Having thought about it, I think it is actually very humane. Their death is essentially instantaneous if done correctly and if an animal is expecting and ready to die anyway (as old or seriously diseased animals generally are) I don't think it is a terrible shock to them. I always make sure they are comfortable and I talk to them and explain what is happening and then just do it.

Not sure if it helps or not but that's my experience.

HV

The strategy I find most useful in stopping those self-inflicted magical attacks is to figure out why I'm doing it to myself. What's the payoff? What attracts me to that self-defeating project? There's always some kind of logic behind it, however shortsighted.

For example, many years ago, I realized that I was setting myself up to fail at every important project I tried as a way to get out of a miserable double bind my parents put me in during my childhood: if I tried my best and succeeded, I got no praise, just a comment that I ought to do that well all the time, and that best then became the minimum acceptable in the future. If I failed, yeah, I got yelled at, but I could deal with that -- and failing deliberately meant that I didn't have the bitter experience of disappointed hope. It also kept me safe from having the terms of the deal changed at the last moment so my success suddenly became a failure, as happened tolerably often.

I had to be ready to realize that, and to accept that I was no longer subject to the whims of my family and had better ways to deal with potential disappointments. Once I got to that point, I could let go of the old pattern fairly easily, and go on to accomplish some things.

comments.title: Who/what was the real Jesus?
comments.content: In The Occult Philosophy Workbook you mention that sometimes higher beings incarnate as humans, come to earth with a particular purpose, accomplish said work, and then often promptly die by violence. This sounds a great deal like Jesus. On the other hand, you have also mentioned that much of what Christians believe about Jesus might be a fabrication of Paul, and that you prefer the gnostic teachings on Christ. So I was wondering if you could give a snap shot of your thoughts on Jesus. Was he just a first century occultist with a knack for exorcism? The divine in human flesh? Something else?

replies.title: Re: Who/what was the real Jesus?
replies.content:
I don't believe that the person we now call Jesus of Nazareth -- Yeshua bin Maryam, as they would have called him back in the day -- was God, in any unique sense. I don't believe he was born of a physically intact virgin without benefit of spermatozoa, I don't believe that he returned to life after being biologically dead for portions of three days, and I don't believe that he physically ascended into heaven. All those things are standard mythological elements from the Hellenistic folk religions of the time, which got loaded onto the memory of a remarkable Galilean teacher and healer by people who, like Paul of Tarsus, never actually met him.

Mind you, it's unquestionably true that people who pray to Jesus Christ very often have those prayers answered. That's true of all gods and goddesses, however, and it's happened rather more than once that invented deities have responded to prayer -- I'm thinking here of the experience of the Reformed Druids of North America, who invented a god of the groves named Dalon ap Landu and started doing rituals to him, and got an answer. For that matter, I've had an uneasy feeling for years now that if I started seriously invoking the gods of the Cthulhu mythos, I'd get an answer, and not a weak one. The universe is a strange place and the Divine seems quite willing to humor us.

As for the historical person Jesus of Nazareth, I don't claim to know what he was or wasn't. I wasn't there at the time, and trying to gauge that from a handful of biased accounts written many decades after the fact is a very dubious proposition. That's about as much as I can say on the subject.

replies.content:

replies.content: I won't say it isn't tempting. There's an early Robert Bloch story that has Druids worshipping the Black Goat of the Woods, too, so it's even got textual justification. ;-) But for the time being, at least, I'll stick to slightly less squamous and rugose Druid deities...

replies.title: Re: Who/what was the real Jesus?
replies.content:
Is it even possible that Jesus of Nazareth's followers created an egregore of him after his death, and a deity, seeing an opportunity, took control of the egregore and impersonated Jesus? That possibility came to my mind mind when you & KS were posting about "Amelia" being the vehicle for a war goddess. (The Jesus in the Synoptic Gospels seemed convinced that the world was going to end soon, so his followers shouldn't worry about their finances or starting a family, just doing what is required to get into the Kingdom of Heaven. If Christians followed his teachings, the religion would have petered out within a couple generations, but the Risen Christ seemed to have somewhat different priorities.)

Patrick H

replies.title: Re: Who/what was the real Jesus?
replies.content: That's one possibility out of many. To my mind, though, Stevan Davies in The Gospel of Thomas and Christian Wisdom makes a very solid case that the Gospel of Thomas, which lacks the apocalyptic business, is closer to the teachings of the historical Jesus than the synoptic gospels, which were heavily edited after 70 AD to fit the apocalyptic frenzy of the time.

replies.title: Re: Who/what was the real Jesus?
replies.content: To be fair, the New Testament is the best-sourced document we have from Antiquity by a long shot, both in volume of sources and their chronological proximity. Still, it's filtered through the initial editing process, the Councils, and the vast gulf of time between the early Roman Empire and today; thus you can't be certain. I also think it's probable that egregores have been piled onto the original Jesus, so that when you invoke that name, there may be someone/something responding to you that you wouldn't expect.

comments.title: Worship and Prayer
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have some questions about prayer and worship.

1) I was raised as an Episcopalian, and never understood the point of worshiping God or Jesus. My thought process has been: Why does God need me to thank him endlessly for creating me?
2) I also don't understand the point of prayer. If I am supposed to believe that everything is God's will, then doesn't it seem to be an act of rebellion to ask God to change something in my life that he didn't intend? And going back to question one, why should I worship a God who has willed upon me such a miserable life?
3) I am no longer an Episcopalian, and I am studying occult philosophy, but when family members keep pushing the above-mentioned Christian doctrines on me, and I'm not sure what to think.
4) Do the non-Christian gods you have mentioned require worship?

replies.title: Re: Worship and Prayer
replies.content:
"Worship" is one of those words that has been debased over the centuries. Its original meaning was, literally, "worthship" -- that is, recognizing something or someone as having worth. It didn't mean groveling or self-abasement. Nor is this something that most gods or goddesses expect, or want. For that matter, I'm not sure it's something that the Christian god actually wants, though he's no doubt had to get used to it by now.

I think a lot of the trouble comes from the use of parental metaphors for the Christian god. It's all "father" this and "father" that, and I suspect that pushes buttons dating back to early childhood in those people who had decent relationships with their parents, at least at that age. The adoring certainty of the one-year-old that Daddy knows everything and can do anything and will wrap his little bunnikins in his loving arms seems to have spilled over into theology.

I didn't have that kind of childhood. My father was mostly absent, and the less said about my mother the better. Thus I don't have those buttons, and all the talk about the Christian god that uses paternal metaphors seems cloying and vaguely fake to me. I gather the same is true for you.

I don't experience deities that way. I encounter them the way I'd encounter older and wiser friends. They don't expect or want me to babble adoringly at them as though I was eighteen months old. Nor do I constantly beg them for favors -- you wouldn't do that to a friend, would you? Prayer, for me, is conversation, and it involves as much listening as talking.

I don't know if you can stand fantasy fiction, much less if you're interested in mine, but so far, at least, the best description I've written of how I see interaction with deities is in my novel The Weird of Hali: Kingsport. In that story the main character, Jenny Parrish, takes up the practice of invoking the Great Old One Tsathoggua -- and he answers. It's very different from the way most Christians describe their religious activities, but it's based on my own experiences. You might possibly find it helpful.

replies.content:
(if I may)

1) It's sort of unclear what the divine powers that answer to human ideas about "God" or "Jesus" are actually doing, since the picture in the religions that produced those ideas doesn't really add up in the way that those religions propose that it should add up. However, acts that put your thoughts on those powers, and that open up space for those powers' influence by putting your thoughts on the way in which those powers (are guessed to) have important virtues that you still need to develop and have them far more profoundly than you do, thereby put you in a state of conscious being that's closer to theirs. This is usually a good thing, at least relative to how much of that that most people do.

(One part of that "usually" is: In Shinto, I've read that they have a nomenclature for dividing a kami (神, "numen" or "awe-presence") into four parts, one of which is the aramitama (荒御魂, something like "untamed-wilds soul"). I find that to be a helpful concept for relating to whatever's going on with how, with any given human idea that the divine powers have invested the ability to contact them into that idea, there will be something like downsides, such that most people other than dedicated monastics and mystics will want to balance those something-like-downsides with the influence of some other divine power. Empirically, the Abrahamic God-the-Father might have an aramitama of a tendency for a kind of purity-obsessed artificially black-and-white subjugation-justifying perspective towards others who don't seem to be part of whatever command hierarchy that one finds oneself in, or towards such parts of oneself. Jesus might work as something of a counterbalance to God's aramitama; this might be one read on part of what's going on with Jesus's mythological attributes of savior and redeemer.)

(Another part of that "usually" is: relationship is a two-way street, and it's important to be aware of the possibility of rebuffs, in one or all respects. The Old Testament presents lots of examples of God putting himself out of the kind of relationship people want when, say, they aren't repenting of something they're doing and so it would bring shame upon his name.)

2) Intercessory prayer seems to have something to do with participating in the ongoing work of creation. It might be a different kind of opening up space for those powers' influence, one that lets that influence flow into the world, the same way devotional prayer opens up space for those powers' influence to flow into your own consciousness and future decisions. To try to pull that idea back into a Christian paradigm with the idea of an omnipotent God, apparently that idea needs to include a part where it is God's will for some details of the universe to be the result of human decisions about praying for some things and not praying for other things.
